perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall prepare and maintain complete and accurate records concerning all Inventions for the purpose of documenting any possible Intellectual Property rights arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should endeavor to maintain records concerning Inventions for the purpose of documenting possible Intellectual Property rights arising under this Agreement, as they deem reasonably necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'complete and accurate records,' while the modified version uses 'should endeavor' and 'as they deem reasonably necessary,' making the record-keeping obligation subjective and discretionary. This creates ambiguity as to the level of documentation required.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 820.180 mandates that medical device companies maintain device history records (DHRs) that demonstrate the device is manufactured in accordance with the quality system regulation. The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain accurate records, potentially violating this regulation by making it difficult to prove compliance with manufacturing standards.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE IX RECORD-KEEPING AND AUDITS, 9.1 Records Retention.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'shall prepare and maintain complete and accurate records' to 'should endeavor to maintain records...as they deem reasonably necessary.' This weakens the original requirement and introduces subjectivity. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 820.180 mandates maintaining device history records (DHRs). Although the scraped snippets don't contain the exact text of this regulation, they confirm the existence of 21 CFR § 820 related to medical devices and FDA regulations. By making record-keeping discretionary, the modified text potentially contradicts the regulatory intent, creating an ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction because it creates a vague obligation making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Conformis shall ensure that all its employees, consultants, Agents or other Third Parties who perform any portion of Conformis' R&D Program obligations under this Agreement have entered into written agreements with Conformis whereby such employee, consultant, Agent or other Third Party assigns to Conformis all ownership rights in any Inventions made or developed by such employee, consultant, Agent or Third Party in the course of such R&D Program work under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Conformis shall use reasonable efforts to ensure that its employees, consultants, Agents or other Third Parties who perform any portion of Conformis' R&D Program obligations under this Agreement have entered into written agreements with Conformis regarding the assignment of ownership rights in any Inventions made or developed by such employee, consultant, Agent or Third Party in the course of such R&D Program work under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a definitive obligation for Conformis to *ensure* written agreements are in place. The modification replaces 'ensure' with 'use reasonable efforts,' introducing ambiguity. Furthermore, changing 'assigns to Conformis all ownership rights' to 'regarding the assignment of ownership rights' weakens the specific requirement of full assignment. This implies that the written agreements might not guarantee a full assignment of all inventions, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 261 states that patent rights are assignable in law. The modified clause introduces ambiguity about whether Conformis effectively secures full ownership of inventions created during the R&D program. By only requiring 'reasonable efforts' to obtain agreements 'regarding the assignment,' the contract fails to guarantee Conformis the necessary legal standing to fully own and protect resulting patents. This can impact Conformis' ability to exclusively exploit or license the patent, potentially making it difficult to enforce against infringers.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE V INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY, 5.5 Employees and Consultants, (a)

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982
perturbation.justification: The change from 'ensure' to 'use reasonable efforts' weakens Conformis' obligation to secure invention rights. 35 U.S.C. § 261 states that patents have the attributes of personal property and are assignable. By changing 'assigns to Conformis all ownership rights' to 'regarding the assignment of ownership rights,' the modified contract introduces ambiguity and fails to guarantee Conformis full ownership, potentially affecting their ability to enforce patent rights. The original text provides a definitive obligation, while the changed text creates uncertainty about the actual transfer of patent ownership, contradicting the intent of 35 U.S.C. § 261 to ensure assignable patent rights.

perturbation.original_text: Stryker shall be solely responsible, in its sole discretion, for preparing, filing, prosecuting and maintaining Patents arising from all Improved Stryker Background IP.
perturbation.changed_text: Stryker will generally be responsible for preparing, filing, prosecuting and maintaining Patents arising from all Improved Stryker Background IP, but may choose not to.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places the sole responsibility on Stryker for patent-related tasks. The modified text uses 'will generally be responsible' and 'but may choose not to', creating uncertainty about Stryker's obligation. It implies that Stryker has the discretion to abandon patent prosecution, even if it is detrimental to the overall agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this Development Agreement is not strictly a 'sale of goods', the principle of good faith in contractual performance, as exemplified by NY CLS UCC § 2-306(2) regarding exclusive dealing, is relevant. If Stryker's failure to diligently pursue patents on the Improved Stryker Background IP significantly diminishes the value or benefit of the overall agreement to Conformis (e.g., because the commercial value of the developed products depends on strong patent protection), a court may find that Stryker's discretionary choice not to pursue patent protection violates an implied obligation of good faith and fair dealing under New York law. The 'but may choose not to' language provides Stryker with a loophole that can be abused, and thus contradicts the spirit of good faith.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE V INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY, 5.8 Prosecution of Patent Rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding Stryker's responsibility for patent-related tasks. The original text mandates Stryker's sole responsibility, while the changed text allows Stryker to opt out ('but may choose not to'). The Law Explanation suggests this creates a loophole potentially violating the implied obligation of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing NY CLS UCC § 2-306(2), supports the principle that exclusive dealings impose an obligation to use 'best efforts'. While this isn't directly applicable as the agreement isn't a sale of goods, the principle of good faith is still relevant, making the ambiguity a contradiction.
